message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Early epidemiological studies report associations of diverse cardiometabolic conditions especially body mass index (BMI), with COVID-19 susceptibility and severity, but causality has not been established. Identifying causal risk factors is critical to inform preventive strategies aimed at modifying disease risk.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We sought to evaluate the causal associations of cardiometabolic conditions with COVID-19 susceptibility and severity.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Two-sample Mendelian Randomization (MR) Study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Population-based cohorts that contributed to the genome-wide association study (GWAS) meta-analysis by the COVID-19 Host Genetics Initiative.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Patients hospitalized with COVID-19 diagnosed by RNA PCR, serologic testing, or clinician diagnosis. Population controls defined as anyone who was not a case in the cohorts.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Selected genetic variants associated with 17 cardiometabolic diseases, including diabetes, coronary artery disease, stroke, chronic kidney disease, and BMI, at
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <5×10
                    <jats:sup>-8</jats:sup>
                    from published largescale GWAS.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcomes</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We performed an inverse-variance weighted averages of variant-specific causal estimates for susceptibility, defined as people who tested positive for COVID-19 vs. population controls, and severity, defined as patients hospitalized with COVID-19 vs. population controls, and repeated the analysis for BMI using effect estimates from UKBB. To estimate direct and indirect causal effects of BMI through obesity-related cardiometabolic diseases, we performed pairwise multivariable MR. We used
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <0.05/17 exposure/2 outcomes=0.0015 to declare statistical significance.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Genetically increased BMI was causally associated with testing positive for COVID-19 [6,696 cases / 1,073,072 controls;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =6.7×10
                    <jats:sup>-4</jats:sup>
                    , odds ratio and 95% confidence interval 1.08 (1.03, 1.13) per kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    ] and a higher risk of COVID-19 hospitalization [3,199 cases/897,488 controls;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =8.7×10
                    <jats:sup>-4</jats:sup>
                    , 1.12 (1.04, 1.21) per kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    ]. In the multivariable MR, the direct effect of BMI was abolished upon conditioning on the effect on type 2 diabetes but persisted when conditioning on the effects on coronary artery disease, stroke, chronic kidney disease, and c-reactive protein. No other cardiometabolic exposures tested were associated with a higher risk of poorer COVID-19 outcomes.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Genetic evidence supports BMI as a causal risk factor for COVID-19 susceptibility and severity. This relationship may be mediated via type 2 diabetes. Obesity may have amplified the disease burden of the COVID-19 pandemic either single-handedly or through its metabolic consequences.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>KEY POINTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Is there a causal association between cardiometabolic conditions and COVID-19 susceptibility or severity?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      Using two-sample Mendelian randomization of 17 cardiometabolic diseases and traits, only body mass index was found to be causally associated with testing positive for COVID-19 (6,696 cases/ 1,073,072 controls;
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      =6.7×10
                      <jats:sup>-4</jats:sup>
                      ) and a higher risk of COVID-19 (3,199 cases/897,488 controls;
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      =8.7×10
                      <jats:sup>-4</jats:sup>
                      ).
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Genetic evidence supports BMI as a causal risk factor for COVID-19 susceptibility and severity.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Cardiometabolic Risk Factors for COVID-19 Susceptibility and Severity: A Mendelian Randomization Analysis
